Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic this semester. I tried to carve out time in the evenings after work, but between my job and other responsibilities, it rarely went as planned. I'd usually end up cramming materials whenever I could find a spare moment—sometimes at lunch, sometimes late at night. I aimed for about 13 hours a week, but the quality of that time varied wildly depending on how exhausted I was.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied based on deadlines and whatever crisis was happening that week. I wanted to be consistent, but life kept throwing curveballs at me. Some weeks I'd barely touch anything, then I'd have these intense bursts of activity when assignments were due. It wasn't ideal, but I was just trying to survive and keep moving forward.
Q3: Usually it was either an assignment deadline looming or getting a notification that something new was posted. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if I'd missed anything important since my attendance was so spotty. The gamified tasks actually helped motivate me sometimes—earning points or badges gave me a small dopamine hit that made logging in feel less like a chore.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. I never quite got to the point where it felt automatic or natural. There were brief periods where I'd check in more regularly, especially when the gamified elements were engaging, but then stress would pile up and I'd fall out of the habit. I wish I could say it became routine, but it always felt like something I had to force myself to do.
Q5: The reading materials and written resources were my lifeline since I learn best through reading and writing. I could download PDFs and work through them at my own pace, highlighting and taking notes. The gamified tasks were surprisingly helpful too—they broke things into smaller, manageable chunks that didn't feel as overwhelming. When I could see my progress visually, it kept me going even when I felt like giving up.
Q6: I basically never touched the discussion forums. With zero posts from me, that probably shows. I just couldn't bring myself to engage publicly when I was already feeling behind and anxious about my performance. The video lectures I'd sometimes skip or speed through because they took too long, and I could get the same information faster by reading. Some supplementary materials felt like busywork, so I'd skip those entirely.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could clearly see how it connected to assignments or exams. The gamified elements helped because they gave immediate feedback and made me feel like I was accomplishing something. If something felt abstract or disconnected from what I actually needed to pass, I'd mentally file it under 'optional' even if it technically wasn't. Time is precious when you're juggling everything I am.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford. Required ones got my attention because they directly impacted my grade, but optional stuff? I'd look at it and think 'that would be nice, but I have three other things due.' There was always this guilt attached to optional activities—like I knew they'd probably help, but the anxiety of falling behind on required work made me skip them.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded almost everything I could and studied offline constantly. I'd print readings and annotate them on the bus or during my lunch break at work. I had a whole notebook full of handwritten summaries and notes that never showed up in any system. Some of my best studying happened with just my notes and a highlighter, completely disconnected from the internet.
Q10: So many times. I'd download a week's worth of readings in one quick session, then spend hours over the next few days actually working through them offline. The LMS would just show a brief login and download, but the real learning happened later with my printed materials. Also, I'd study with peers in person—we had 44 interactions tracked, but even those don't capture the depth of our study sessions at the library.
Q11: All the time. I'd grab the PDFs or lecture slides in one go, then work through them over several days without logging back in. It was more practical for me since I didn't always have reliable internet access or the mental energy to navigate the platform. Once I had the materials saved locally, I could study anywhere—on the train, during breaks, wherever.
Q12: There were definitely dark periods, especially mid-semester when my stress was through the roof. I was dealing with high anxiety and feeling completely overwhelmed by everything piling up. My attendance dropped because I just couldn't face another lecture when I was already so behind. The boredom set in too—when you're stressed and anxious, even important material starts feeling tedious and pointless.
Q13: Massively. When work got demanding or I had personal stuff going on, the LMS was the first thing to suffer. I'd log in with good intentions but find myself just staring at the screen, too mentally exhausted to actually process anything. Confusion about what to prioritize made me freeze up sometimes—I'd have multiple things due and just shut down instead of tackling any of them.
Q14: More often than I'd like to admit. I'd have materials open, maybe even scroll through them, but my brain was somewhere else entirely. Sometimes I was too anxious to actually absorb anything—I'd read the same paragraph three times without retaining a word. Those sessions probably looked productive in the logs, but they were basically wasted time.
Q15: The exam prep is the big one I avoided, and my score shows it. I kept telling myself I'd get to it, but the anxiety around it was paralyzing. It felt so high-stakes that I'd procrastinate by doing smaller assignments instead—at least those I could complete and feel good about. Looking back, I was self-sabotaging, but in the moment, avoidance felt like the only way to cope with the overwhelming dread.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my entire motivation system. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly find the energy to engage that I couldn't muster otherwise. My LMS activity probably spiked dramatically in the 24-48 hours before anything was due. It wasn't healthy, but the pressure was the only thing that could cut through the boredom and anxiety enough to make me actually do the work.
Q17: When I got good feedback on assignments—and my assignment scores were actually pretty decent—it gave me a boost of confidence. Those moments reminded me I was capable, even when everything else felt like it was falling apart. But feedback on exams was demoralizing, and I'd avoid looking at it because I knew it would just confirm my fears about not being good enough.
Q18: The structure felt overwhelming at times, honestly. Having 20 online courses worth of content to navigate meant I was constantly context-switching and losing track of where I was. The gamified elements helped break things down, but the overall volume was just too much. A more streamlined structure with clearer priorities would have helped me focus my limited energy better.
Q19: The gamification definitely made engagement easier—having clear milestones and rewards appealed to my need for tangible progress. But the sheer amount of content and the lack of clear prioritization made it harder. When everything seems equally important, nothing feels urgent until it's too late. Better scaffolding or suggested study paths would have helped someone like me who was struggling to keep up.
Q20: I think that's exactly what happened to me with exam prep. I was active in the system, accessing materials, doing the gamified tasks, but I wasn't converting that activity into the deep studying needed for exams. Sometimes high activity is just anxious clicking around, looking for something manageable to do instead of tackling the hard stuff. Activity doesn't equal productive engagement.
Q21: That makes sense to me because so much of my real learning happened offline. Someone could download everything once, study intensively with printed materials, and ace the course without ever logging back in. The LMS only captures one slice of the learning process. I wish my offline effort had translated better to my exam performance, but I understand how someone could succeed that way.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you're active, what you're clicking on, how long you're logged in. But they completely miss the quality of that engagement and all the offline work. They can't tell if you're actually understanding the material or just going through the motions. They also miss the emotional context—whether you're engaged because you're interested or just panicking about a deadline.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting genuine mental effort into understanding it. It's not just showing up or clicking through materials—it's wrestling with ideas, making connections, and wanting to know more. For me, engagement also means feeling capable and not completely overwhelmed. When my anxiety was manageable, I could engage deeply. When it wasn't, I was just surviving.
Q24: The LMS would never show how hard I fought to keep going despite everything. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent with printed notes on the bus, the study sessions with classmates that kept me sane, or the genuine effort behind my assignment scores. It also wouldn't show the anxiety attacks before exams or the way boredom masked deeper feelings of being overwhelmed. My data looks like disengagement, but it was actually a constant struggle to stay afloat.
